segments.title: Corbett’s Drake books and strategic history
segments.summary: Corbett’s studies of Drake turn Elizabethan sea power into historical case studies of British national strategy.
segments.body: The excerpt presents Corbett’s Drake books as more than biographies or accounts of ships and battles. By linking Elizabethan conflict, trade protection, and later strategic anxieties, he used the past to explain why naval mastery mattered to contemporary Britain. The books also helped him develop a language for officers studying strategy professionally.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Corbett's Drake studies, their strategic links, and their value as national-strategy case studies.

segments.title: Strategy, policy, and operations beyond battle narratives
segments.summary: Corbett’s approach shifts naval history from ships and battles toward the interaction of national strategy, policy, and operations.
segments.body: This moment identifies the central change in Corbett’s historical approach: naval events become part of a wider national effort. It is a useful starting point for understanding why a campaign cannot be judged solely by tactical action at sea. Policy aims and operational choices shape one another throughout the argument.

evidence.note: The transcript says the studies move from ships and battle to the interplay of national strategy, policy, and operations.

segments.title: Corbett’s early strengths and limitations
segments.summary: The trailer weighs Corbett’s forensic insight and judgment against an early tendency toward hero-centred history and incomplete strategic analysis.
segments.body: The assessment does not treat Corbett’s early work as fully formed strategic theory. It credits his ability to ask new questions and judge evidence while noting the limits of leader-focused narrative and his still-developing grasp of higher direction in war. Those books nevertheless established the foundation for his later career.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Corbett's weaknesses as well as his forensic insight, judgment, and importance to his later career.

segments.title: Archival research and the higher direction of war
segments.summary: Corbett’s later method uses archival research to connect naval figures with the statesmen, policy, and imperial context that direct war.
segments.body: Corbett’s next step required detailed archival research into how policy and strategy were made, rather than treating commanders as isolated heroic figures. The trailer places admirals alongside political leaders and connects land operations, empire, and global power to naval strategy. It frames his work as a systemic account of the British way of war that could inform debate with both statesmen and soldiers.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Corbett's archival approach, its attention to policy and statesmen, and its systemic view of British war-making.
